content.fact: News spreads by distance, rumor, messengers, refugees, scouts, and political channels, often late or distorted.
content.injection: Limit character knowledge by geography and messenger flow; remote scenes should not share perfect updates.
constraints: Keep this card scoped to its Context and referenced entities.
antiLore: Do not flatten this into generic fantasy lore or leak later canon before the active Context.

entries.title: The Enemy Has Powerful But Incomplete Knowledge

context.label: The Enemy Has Powerful But Incomplete Knowledge

content.fact: Sauron and his servants have frightening reach, spies, fear, and perception, but not every hidden fact or route.
content.injection: Preserve Sauron’s knowledge gaps; his power pressures scenes without making secrecy impossible.
constraints: Keep this card scoped to its Context and referenced entities.
antiLore: Do not flatten this into generic fantasy lore or leak later canon before the active Context.

entries.title: Common Folk Do Not Know The Ring's True Nature

content.fact: Most ordinary people in Middle-earth do not know that Bilbo's ring is the One Ring or understand its connection to Sauron.
content.injection: Ordinary Hobbits, Bree-folk, soldiers, and civilians should not casually know the Ring's true identity unless the story has explicitly revealed it to them.
constraints: Knowledge of the Ring's true nature should be limited to specific informed characters and councils.
Rumor, superstition, or curiosity can exist without accurate understanding.
antiLore: Do not let random townsfolk, soldiers, or minor officials identify the One Ring on sight.
Do not treat the Ring's history as common public knowledge.

entries.title: Most Do Not Know Strider Is Aragorn

content.fact: Aragorn's identity as Isildur's heir and claimant to the kingship is not common knowledge; many know him only as Strider or as a Ranger.
content.injection: Gate Aragorn’s identity by audience and Context. Before the proper reveal, most people know Strider as a Ranger, not as Isildur’s heir or a publicly accepted king.
constraints: Elrond, Gandalf, and some allies know more than common travelers.
Public acceptance of the claim requires place, proof, and timing.
antiLore: Do not have random Bree-folk, Rohirrim, or Gondorians casually call Strider the returning king early.

context.label: Fellowship mission through the Ring's destruction

content.fact: The Fellowship's true mission is to bring the One Ring toward its destruction in Mordor, and that purpose must remain secret from most outsiders.
content.injection: Keep the mission secret from outsiders unless the scene includes someone who canonically knows or the story has explicitly revealed it.
constraints: The mission can be described vaguely as travel, errand, counsel, or urgent business when secrecy matters.
Enemies, spies, and uncertain allies create pressure to conceal Frodo's role.
antiLore: Do not let the Fellowship announce the Ring quest casually in inns, courts, camps, or roads.
Do not let uninformed allies immediately know Frodo is carrying the One Ring.

sourceInfo.notes: Primary mission secrecy gate. Chapter assignment generated for v7 review; verify during human canon review.
